content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
January 2, 1998
Mr. Richard Smith
Campaign Chairman
4261 Jacobus Drive
Garden Valley, California 95633
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-573
Dear Mr. Smith:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Because you have not disclosed the identity of the person or
persons for whom you seek this advice, we are treating this as a request for informal advice, as
required under Regulation 18329.2
QUESTIONS
1. Are individuals, appointed by an incumbent supervisor, who sit on boards and
committees that are only advisory in nature and would require a majority vote ofthe board of
supervisors to act upon their advice prohibited from making contributions to the person(s) who
appointed them under Government Code Section 85705?
2. Would individuals, appointed by another supervisor, other than the candidate, but
approved by a majority of the supervisors, including the candidate, be prohibited from making
contributions to the person(s) who appointed them under Government Code Section 85705?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Government Code Section 85705 applies to individuals who sit on boards and
committees if the boards and committees have decision-making authority.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-573
Page 2
2. If the entire board of supervisors are required to make the appointment and ifthe
boards and committees have decision-making authority, as defined in Regulation 1 8700(a)(l).
Section 85705 would apply to the individuals who sit on the boards and committees.
FACTS
Your candidate is one of five county supervisors who is running for re-election to the
same office. Your candidate has a controlled committee. You are seeking advice regarding
Section 85705 and how that section affects your candidate.
ANALYSIS
The contribution prohibitions in Government Code section 85705 will apply to any
government appointee who holds a position with the authority to make governmental decisions.
(Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-97-203.)
Section 85705, which was added to the Act by Proposition 208, provides as follows:
“No person appointed to a public board or commission or as Trustee ofthe
California State University or Regent of the University of California during tenure
in office shall donate to, or solicit or accept any campaign contribution for, any
committee controlled by the person who made the appointment to that office or
any other entity with the intent that the recipient ofthe donation be any committee
controlled by such person who made the appointment.”
To determine whether an appointee is a member of a board or similar body authorized to
make governmental decisions, we look to Regulation 1 8700(a)(1), which furnishes reasonable,
objective criteria:
“A board or commission possesses decision making authority
whenever:
(A) It may make a final governmental decision;
(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
overridden; or
(C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an
extendedperiod oftime have been, regularly approved without
signflcant amendment or modification by another public official
or governmental agency.” (Emphasis added.)


File No. 1-97-573
Page 3
Thus, if any appointed board, commission, committee or similar body, whether created by
statute, ordinance or city council resolution, has “decision-making authority” as defined in
Regulation 18700(a)(l), appointments to that body are governed by section 85705. (Guinan
Advice Letter, No. 1-97-305.) For purposes of analysis of Section 85705, it is important to
analyze whether the boards and committees are truly only advisory in nature. If any of the
criteria of Regulation 1 8700(a)(1) are satisfied, Section 85705 would apply to the appointees
who sit on the boards and committees.
Each member of the boards and committees is selected and nominated by an individual
member of the board of supervisors, but a majority vote of the entire board is necessary to make
the appointment. “Person” as defmed in the Act means not only an individual but also “any
organization or group of persons acting in concert.” (Section 82047.) Therefore, the “person
who made the appointment,” as referred to in Section 85705, is the entire board of supervisors
since a member of the board of supervisors nominates a potential board or committee member
and a majority vote of the board of supervisors is necessary to make the appointment. Thus, if
the board or committee has decision-making authority, as defined in Regulation 18700(a)(l), no
person appointed to these boards and committees may donate to, solicit or accept any campaign
contributions for any committee controlled by any member of the board of supervisors.
(Vasquez-Connolly, supra, and Abdelnour Advice Letter, No. A-97-164, Petzold Advice Letter,
No. 1-97-2 12, copies enclosed.)
We have reached the opposite conclusion in situations where the mayor appoints an
individual to a public board or commission and the appointment is subject to ratification by the
city council. We have concluded that the mayor is the only person who made the appointment
and to whom the prohibition in Section 85705 applies. (Abdelnour Advice Letter, supra, Lyions
supra, copies enclosed.) Since, under your facts, it appears that nominations are made by more
than one supervisor and then a majority vote is required by the entire board of supervisors, the
nominating supervisor is not making the appointment.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
LO
By: l Lynda Doherty
Consultant, Legal Division
SGC:LD:jlw
Enclosures

2. Would individuals, appointed by another supervisor, other than the candidate, but
approved by a majority of the supervisors, including the candidate, be prohibited from making
contributions to the person(s) who appointed them under Government Code Section 85705?

2. If the entire board of supervisors are required to make the appointment and ifthe
